The ``get_registration_fields`` method does not at all account for 

* the possibility that the server returns a data form instead of the classic registration fields (XEP-0077 is a historically-grown spec which didn’t age too well, unfortunately).
* the possibility that the server only returns ``<instructions/>`` for an out-of-band registration.

The code should at least detect those cases and raise an error.

By returning the XSO instead of attempting to parse it, it’s however possible to circumvent this necessity.

I’d at least add a ``notify`` property where notification can be turned on and off. Maybe even enabling/disabling the exporting of the feature should be possible.

I would even go that far that the RegisteredPEPNode should be the object used within the PEPService and all normal interfacing which is done through PEPService for enabling/disabling notify etc. should simply be a forward to the RegisteredPEPNode. It should also be possible to obtain the RegisteredPEPNode when claiming a namespace so that callbacks can be used without the descriptor (useful if you implement a PEP application in the application layer, without services).

Using the descriptor then is just a simple interface to obtain the RegisteredPEPNode.

We do something a little more clever? but it quickly becomes unreadable :)

```
import re

r1 = re.compile('(\(default.*\))$')


r2 = re.compile('(\(default ('
               '[^(\'"]*'
               '|'
               '\([^(]*\)' # tuple
               '|'
               r'("(?:[^"\\]|\\.)*")'  # double quoted string
               '|'
               r"|('(?:[^'\\]|\\.)*')"  # single quoted string
               ')\))')

r3 = re.compile('(\(default ('
               '[^(\'"]*'
               '|'
               '\((' # tuple start
               '[^(\'"]*'
               '|'
               r'("(?:[^"\\]|\\.)*")'  # double quoted string
               '|'
               r"|('(?:[^'\\]|\\.)*')"  # single quoted string
               ')*\)' # tuple end
               '|'
               r'("(?:[^"\\]|\\.)*")'  # double quoted string
               '|'
               r"|('(?:[^'\\]|\\.)*')"  # single quoted string
               ')\))')


items = [
    '(default "moo")',
    '(default "moo") ',
    '(default ("moo"))',
    '(default ("m(oo"))',
    '(default ("m(oo", \'moo\'))',
    '(default ("moo",)',
    '(default ("mo)o",)',
    '(default "mo(o")',
]

for item in items:
    p1 = bool(r1.match(item))
    p2 = bool(r2.match(item))
    p3 = bool(r3.match(item))
    print(item, p1, p2, p3)
```

gives

```
(default "moo") True True True
(default "moo")  False True True
(default ("moo")) True True True
(default ("m(oo")) True False True
(default ("m(oo", 'moo')) True False True
(default ("moo",) True False False
(default ("mo)o",) True False False
(default "mo(o") True True True
```

Unfortunately it doesn't promise compatibility. Generally, I've avoided pinning in library packages unless there's a known version of a dependency that breaks things.  This allows apps to pin their dependencies as needed.

Funnily enough, this is how we found this issue in the first place. We had an internal package pinning `tldextract>1.2` and an internal application pinning `requests==2.2`. `tldextract` auto-updated on a release and we ran in to a hell of version conflicts.

I just did another test run locally with requests==2.0.1 and it failed. I'll bump the pin to be >=2.1.0 since that's the lowest known working version. I'm of the opinion that leaving the upper-end unrestricted (i.e. don't pin requests<3) is alright, since it's not yet known to fail.

I personally don't think that the class name in the message adds value here as it is still in the full traceback. I would recommend having the message be targeted for human consumption. ```python
```python
logger.exception(

    check_name,
    message,
    extra={'exc_class': e.__class__.__name__}
)
```

New `***_one/many` methods are using completely different protocol depending on whether current write-concern is acknowledged or not. If it is acknowledged, they are using new db commands which were introduced in MongoDB 2.6. When unacknowledged, they are using good old OP_INSERT/OP_UPDATE/... messages. So for each query methods we are testing ack'ed and unack'ed separately because message format and also additional flags handling (upsert, ordered, ...) are completely different.

@pongad , INTERNAL is certainly a complicated status to handle, as @jba clearly points out,  internal errors must be caused by invariants which usually surface by inputs that were not expected by the system and will permanently fail; a bug in the system. However we often see *internal* errors that surface just because an unexpected failure coming from an upstream dependency or an environment issue that usually gets resolved by itself; a transient error, such errors should genuinely be reported as unavailable errors but given our quite complex infrastructure, that is not always the case. Hence, in the pubsub library we have opted to a more conservative approach of retrying even on internal errors.

1. Changing source code on disk would be pretty difficult (or should be).  If you could do it, you've already got access to their PC so you've got free reign anyways.
2. Extensions by their very nature make it technically much easier to get people to run untrusted, malicious code.
3. Allowing extensions to override trusted CLI commands now makes it **even easier** to engineer a social attack because the message isn't "install my sketchy extension" but "install these great improvements to the VM commands". 

I like Troy's idea of a warning when running any overridden commands. I would also suggest considering a strong warning when an extension install would override any CLI commands and a prompt that yes, you want to install this potentially dangerous thing.

2. In cases of non-even division, the initial chunk size should be 1 + the division result, so that you get a decent-sized remainder.

```python
>>> _get_chunks((4, 4), 4)
(2, 2)
>>> _get_chunks((4, 4), 2)
(2, 2)
>>> _get_chunks((5, 5), 2)
(3, 3)
```

```python
>>> _get_chunks((2, 4), 2)
```

It's generally bad practice to test for Python 3 since it means that once Python 4 comes out the Python 2 codepath will be taken (which is almost certainly wrong). That's why you should check for Python 2 and do any special handling there. If you need to do something on Python 3, use `not six.PY2` to make sure it works on Python 4+ as well.

In fact, your else is precisely what I wanted to avoid: break the test on Python 4 although it could very well be the case that it runs fine on that Python version. In summary:

1. Do `if six.PY2: ... else: ...`.
2. Do `if not six.PY2: long = int`

`attrs_iter` is an iterable of dicts for two reasons:

* The base class, `_BaseTestCase`, assumes that an `attrs_iter` class attribute is present.
* Many other classes do the same, and adopting the same convention here allows people familiar with patterns in Pulp Smash more easily understand this code.

In this particular test case, `attrs_iter` only contains one dict. That's something you can take advantage of, and we already do that a few lines up by writing `cls.attrs_iter[0]['_href']`. With that in mind, try this out:

```python
    @classmethod
    def setUpClass(cls):
        """Create an RPM repository with a valid feed and sync it."""
        super(SyncValidFeedTestCase, cls).setUpClass()
        body = _gen_rpm_repo_body()
        body['importer_config']['feed'] = _VALID_FEED
        repo_href = create_repository(cls.cfg, body)['_href']
        cls.sync_repo = []  # raw responses
        report = sync_repository(cls.cfg, repo_href, cls.sync_repo)
        cls.task_bodies = tuple(poll_spawned_tasks(cls.cfg, report))
        cls.attrs_iter = (get(cls.cfg, repo_href),)  # see parent class
```

This is a surprising python gotcha, but one should never use `()` or []` as the default value for a keyword argument. Everytime this method is called, you don't get a new empty tuple, but end up re-using the same tuple from previous calls. It is recommended to instead:
```
def show_logs(self, selected_node_names=None):

    if selected_node_names is None:
        selected_node_names = ()
....
```

This is a change in behavior. Previously, when no matching server block was found we'd exit because we didn't know what addresses to use when setting up the challenge. Now we try performing the challenge using all IPv4 interfaces and port 443. This is probably fine, however, I'd add a `logger.info` message explaining this so users have a better idea what may have gone wrong if they fail the challenge. (If you're curious why I think this should be a `logger.info` message rather than using a higher logging level, see #5139).

Also, Apache attempts to ask the user which vhost to use when it encounters this case. If it's unable to ask the user because Certbot is being run non-interactively, it does the same thing as what we're doing here. It certainly doesn't have to be in this PR, but it might not be a bad idea to do the same thing here in the future.
